data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Wall Street this week was headed for a solid loss, weighed down by a rout in technology stocks and disappointing retail earnings. However, a dovish signal from Federal Reserve chair Jerome Powell on Friday at the annual Jackson Hole Economic Policy Symposium sparked a big rally, helping the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) eke out gains for the week.

While the S&P and also the blue-chip Dow (DJI) notched weekly gains, the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) posted a decline, as cautious investors ahead of Jackson Hole sold off pricey megacap technology stocks. Meanwhile, retail giants Walmart (WMT) and Target (TGT) slid after their quarterly results, with the latter also naming a new chief executive.

Anticipation ahead of Powell's speech was high, with market participants hoping for comments on easing monetary policy. Though Powell did not fully commit to cutting interest rates, he noted that the downside risks to the labor market were increasing and that the "baseline outlook and the shifting balance of risks may warrant adjusting our policy stance."

For the week, the S&P (SP500) added +0.3%, while the Dow (DJI) gained +1.5%. The Nasdaq (COMP:IND) slipped -0.6%. Read a preview of next week's major events in Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch.

data.title: LyondellBassell: Dividend Investors Proceed With Caution, High Probability Of A Dividend Cut
data.description:
Introduction

Over the past few years, the macro environment has placed downward pressure on businesses, mostly due to rising costs as a result of higher interest rates to combat inflation.

Some industries have been impacted more so than others, leading to a steep decline in their share prices. One of those has been LyondellBasell Industries (LYB), a chemical company that has a long operating history dating back to the 1950's and operations all over the globe.

Despite downward pressure on their cash flows, management seems committed to maintaining its current dividend, which I believe could be in jeopardy of being cut in the near future.

In this article, I discuss LyondellBasell's latest earnings, fundamentals, and why, despite their current attractive dividend yield of 10%, investors should proceed with caution as a dividend cut could be in the not-too-distant future.

Brief Overview

As previously mentioned, LYB has a long history dating back to the 1950's, going public only 15 years ago. The company operates in six segments and manufactures products for a wide array of applications like food, transportation, and healthcare to name a few.

Their segments include: Olefins & Polyolefins - Americas, Olefins & Polyolefins - Europe, Asia, & International, Intermediates & Derivatives, Advanced Polymer Solutions, Technology, & Refining.

Latest Quarter

LyondellBasell Industries reported their Q2 earnings at the beginning of the month with a large miss on their bottom line. EPS amounted to $0.62, missing analysts' estimates by a sizable $0.16. The miss was also a testament to the headwinds the chemical industry has faced in recent years.

And LyondellBasell's share price has been a direct reflection of this, down over 43% at the time of writing. And this is because of the steep decline in financials the chemical maker has experienced year-over-year, a result of persistent headwinds like rising energy & feedstock costs.

The decline in their bottom line was significant as the previous year's quarter saw EPS of $2.24, more than a 72% decline, offsetting the increase from $0.33 in the prior quarter.

Not surprisingly, sales also suffered a steep decline as the sector has been plagued by rising costs as well as weakness in construction & automotive.

This is also evident by the significant decline their peer Dow Inc. (DOW) saw in their top and bottom lines, although not as steep. Dow experienced a 38% EPS decline year-over-year from $0.68 to $0.42, while their sales also declined 9.0%.

LYB's sales retracted double-digits from $10.558 billion the previous year's quarter to approximately $7.66 billion. However, their segments did see positives in the quarter due to lower costs, which slightly improved margins. But investors/main question is likely: How safe is the dividend?

Deteriorating Cash Flows

As solely a dividend-focused investor, cash flows are vital during my research. While many investors pay attention to a company's earnings payout ratio, the free cash flow payout ratio should be the preferred metric to look at when assessing dividend safety. But with the large decline in earnings, LYB's earnings payout ratio is well-above 100%.

However, management remains committed to the dividend with the 2.2% raise in May to $1.37 a share, currently putting the earnings payout ratio above 150%.

Free cash flow has continued erode since 2021 as a result of headwinds. Since the end of 2021, free cash flow has declined from approximately $7 billion to $2 billion in 2024. And this has been a result of cash from operations declining.

At the end of 2021, cash from operations was $7.6 billion, while free cash flow amounted to $5.7 billion. At the end of 2024, cash from operations declined to $3.8 billion, while free cash flow was $2 billion.

And while LyondellBasell sufficiently covered the dividend last year, their free cash flow payout ratio has deteriorated as a result of the decline. Paying out $1.7 billion in dividends in 2024, their free cash flow payout ratio of 85% gave them little room for error.

Moreover, in the most recent quarter cash flows have continued to decline. Cash from operations stood at just $359 million, while they paid out $536 million in dividend and buybacks.

Although, this was an improvement was the prior quarter that saw negative cash flows due to additional tax payments. Year-to-date, cash from operations was $230 million, down significantly from $1.2 billion last year.

Capital Allocation Strategy

Going forward, management stated they don't plan to repurchase any additional shares for the remainder of the year and for 2026. Aside from this, management has already reduced CAPEX by putting certain growth projects on hold.

This year they expect CAPEX to be $200 million lower, now expected to be $1.7 billion, driving incremental cash flow growth of $600 million. They also expect to reduce additional operations costs and sell some of their European assets next year. And while these are all great efforts, macro uncertainty should keep investors cautious.

Can The Balance Sheet Support Dividend Sustainability?

During the quarter, management seemed optimistic of their dividend sustainability, one reason being due to their available liquidity. This amounted to $1.7 billion in cash on hand for a total of $6.35 billion including revolver capacity.

And it seems as if cash has been used to sustain the dividend so far. Cash on hand at the end of 2024 was $3.4 billion, while leverage stood at 1.8x. During the quarter net debt to EBITDA was 3.2x, so their leverage ratio has been rising and is something investors should keep a close eye on going forward. But the question is: How much cash will they burn through to sustain the dividend?

Tariffs could also show up in the back half of the year. If so, this could cause costs to rise further, forcing management to cut the dividend, similar to peer Dow Inc, which I discussed last month in an article you can read here.

Valuation

As a result of the sharp price decline in the past year, LyondellBasell's current yield sits at 10% at the time of writing which investors may consider attractive.

But using their earnings projection of $2.69 for the year, their forward P/E of 20.29x still seems expensive for a company facing a plethora of headwinds and at high risk of a dividend cut.

I agree with Wall Street's current hold rating and think investors should wait to see sequential improvements in cash flows before considering investing here.

Risks & Conclusion

As a result of current headwinds, LyondellBasell Industries is a high-risk stock that could suffer more downside, especially if they cut the dividend.

While I do believe management is committed to the dividend, showing confidence with the recent increase in May, further economic weakening could force their hand despite their capital allocation strategy.

And while lower interest rates will likely benefit them, macro uncertainty and current cash flow issues leads me to believe they have a high probability of seeing a dividend cut in the next 6 to 12 months.

If so, they could see further downside. As a result of LyondellBassell's high chances of a dividend cut and macro uncertainty, I rate them a hold here.

The performance data shown represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Returns for periods shorter than one year are cumulative, and results for other share classes will vary. Shares will fluctuate in value and, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance cited. Performance figures assume that all distributions are reinvested. For the most recent month-end performance, visit jhinvestments.com.

Market review and outlook

The second quarter began with a shock to financial markets: a U.S. tariff policy announcement that was significantly more aggressive than expected. Volatility spiked, stock prices plunged, bond yields fell across the yield curve, and credit spreads widened.

Within a week, however, the U.S. administration changed tack with a 90-day pause on new tariffs, providing a much-needed reprieve for the markets. Stock prices sharply rebounded and ended the quarter at a record high. Meanwhile,

U.S. Treasury yields rose, and by the end of June, credit spreads had narrowed significantly.

As of quarter end, corporate earnings expectations declined while the valuation of the stock market based on expected earnings increased. Trade remained a source of uncertainty, as investors assessed the impact of tariffs on inflation, economic growth, and the U.S. Federal Reserve’s interest rate policy. Combined with increased geopolitical tensions and uncertainty surrounding future rate cuts, we believe this backdrop warrants additional caution.

Despite the uncertain market environment, we expect the outperformance opportunity to be greater from individual security selection than from general market exposure. Given highly fluid near-term sentiment, our strategy is to maintain a steady, long-term outlook.

The fund’s equity portfolio outperformed due to favorable security selection across most sectors, especially in information technology. In contrast, sector positioning, which derives from our bottom-up security selection process, was negative, especially in healthcare and energy.

Individually, the largest relative contributor this quarter was Broadcom, Inc., a semiconductor company whose shares rose amid optimism about the company’s opportunity to benefit from the burgeoning AI market. Confidence in spending on AI also lifted shares of a second fund holding, chipmaker Micron Technology, Inc. A third relative contributor was the fund’s relative underweight in consumer electronics company Apple, Inc., a poor performer this quarter that was sold from the portfolio before period end.

In contrast, an underweight in AI chipmaker NVIDIA Corp. detracted, as the company’s shares rose sharply this period. Another performance challenge was an overweight in drugmaker Eli Lilly & Company, whose stock lagged amid uncertainty about drug pricing, including potential tariffs on pharmaceutical products. Lacking exposure to strong-performing benchmark component Meta Platforms, Inc., parent company of Facebook and Instagram, also contributed.

For the fixed-income portfolio, which delivered a positive return that outperformed the index, an out-of-benchmark allocation to high-yield bonds contributed, as did smaller overweights in investment-grade corporate bonds and other credit sectors. However, a sizable overweight in mortgage-backed securities detracted.

Contributors and detractors

The fund’s equity portfolio benefited from strong stock selection across a range of sectors, with notable outperformance in consumer discretionary and technology. Our bottom-up approach to security selection was especially effective in identifying opportunities within the software and semiconductor industries.

Among individual holdings, the top relative contributor this period was Alphabet Inc., whose shares gained on solid advertising revenues and continued strength in cloud services. Another key contributor was a leading electric vehicle manufacturer, which rebounded sharply on better-than-expected delivery volumes. Additionally, our position in a global payments company added value, as it benefited from increased transaction volumes and positive earnings momentum.

Conversely, the portfolio’s underweight in a leading energy firm detracted, as the stock rallied with rising oil prices. A holding in a regional bank also underperformed, driven by weaker loan growth and regulatory concerns. Exposure to a consumer staples company was another negative, as the company faced pricing pressure and softer demand.

Within the fixed-income segment, performance was modestly positive. Outperformance was driven by an underweight to long-duration Treasuries, which lagged amid rising rates. Tactical allocations to corporate bonds and emerging market debt also contributed. However, a concentrated position in agency mortgage-backed securities weighed on results as spreads widened late in the period.

Portfolio changes

The fund’s equity and fixed-income allocations finished June at about 57% and 43%, respectively, compared with 58% and 42%, respectively, on March 31.

In the equity portfolio, we established four new positions and sold two stocks from the fund. Turnover remained somewhat elevated, reflecting various attractive valuation opportunities we saw in Q2.

In the fixed-income portfolio, we increased the fund’s allocation to agency mortgage-backed securities, with smaller additions in asset-backed securities and commercial mortgage-backed securities. We funded these moves by reducing the portfolio’s weightings in investment-grade corporate bonds and U.S. Treasuries.

Footnotes

1 Listed holdings reflect the largest portions of the fund’s total and may change at any time. They are not recommendations to buy or sell any security. Data is expressed as a percentage of net assets and excludes cash and cash equivalents.

2 It is not possible to invest in an index. Index definitions can be found within the "What you should know before Investing" section on the last page of this fact sheet.

3“Net (what you pay)” represents the effect of a contractual fee waiver and/or expense reimbursement and is subject to change.

Original Post

Nvidia, the world's first $4 trillion company (Microsoft (MSFT) is the 2nd), has single-handedly driven U.S. market exceptionalism since the AI boom began in late 2022.

But according to the Financial Times, if we pull Nvidia out of the S&P 500, the index's total returns since 2022 underperform the eurozone's stock benchmark, the MSCI EMU Index, since the bull market began in late 2022." Seeking Alpha.

Nvidia isn't just my largest holding; it's the most significant holding for most investors, including those who own the S&P 500.

I've recommended Nvidia nine times in total, with seven of those recommendations made during the AI boom.

Nvidia is my third-highest returning recommendation since I began writing for Seeking Alpha in June 2016.

And, of course, Nvidia has made the ZEUS LEGACY Family charity hedge fund (which comprises 100% of my life savings in this portfolio) a significant amount of money.

28% of ZEUS's total profits to date are from Nvidia, which is our single largest holding.

That showcases how winners always "punch above their weight."

How much so?

The biggest winners always generate the most gains.

Did you know that from 1926 to 2022, 5% of all the profits in the entire stock market were from Apple (AAPL)?

The top 10 stocks accounted for 21% of all stock market profit.

96% of individual stocks over nearly 100 years didn't beat risk-free cash.

58% of all stocks over 100 years, lost money.

That's why 95% of fund managers can't match or beat their respective benchmarks over time.

And why the #1 fund manager of the last 21 years beat the market by 1.8% CAGR.

And that fund manager was losing to the market in 8 of those years!

The Risks Of Owning Individual Stocks Are Huge

If you don't own the right stocks, you can't earn the market's gains, and if you own the wrong ones?

The wrong stocks, like dividend cutters? Lost 91.5% of their value, adjusted for inflation, during a time when the S&P went up 46X (6.2X adjusted for inflation), dividend stocks went up 97X (13X adjusted for inflation), and dividend growth stocks went up 159X (21.5X adjusted for inflation).

  • Dividend Growth stocks outperformed dividend cutters by 253 times over the 50 years.

And that brings us to the question that Nvidia investors want to know.

Is the thesis still intact? Is Nvidia a long-term buy ahead of earnings on August 27th, even after an incredible run?

And here's Nvidia: since the market bottomed in 2022, marking the beginning of the modern AI boom.

Up 15.2X! It has to be in a bubble! Right?!

Let me tell you the X reasons I recently bought Nvidia ahead of its earnings, and you might want to consider doing the same.

Reason 1: Nvidia's "Bubble" Is 100% Justified

No way a stock can increase 15 times in three years and not be in a bubble, right?

Earnings up 13X, exactly matching price gains, and free cash flow up 15X.

This chart disproves the argument that Nvidia is in a bubble.

That the $4 trillion market cap wasn't justified.

If a stock has 15 times free cash flow growth and increases to 13 times, that is not a bubble. Case closed.

The only argument is whether the current profits and free cash flow will continue to grow or collapse, because AI is in an unsustainable bubble, and these kinds of sales will likely evaporate and possibly never materialize again.

Reason 2: The Data Says This AI Bubble Isn't A Bubble

  • 10 Reasons Why This AI Boom Is Not A Bubble And Makes Every Dip A Gift.

I did a deep dive on the AI boom last week, but here are two of the most important charts from that report.

The level of spending as a % of GDP is similar to the tech bubble.

However, that is only 1/5th of the biggest growth spending ever seen by the U.S., the railroad boom, which saw almost two-thirds of the entire market capitalization go towards one industry.

If the AI revolution plays out as some (the IEA especially) believe, then U.S. tech stocks might see their share of U.S. market cap grow even higher.

The five largest cloud computing giants (Microsoft, Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL), Meta Platforms (META), and Oracle (ORCL)) are expected (based on a FactSet consensus of hundreds of the 75+ analysts covering these companies) to increase their total capital expenditure (Capex + R&D) from $601 billion this year to $700 billion next year.

And then continue growing it at a rate of around 14% CAGR until it reaches $854 billion by 2030.

That's a cumulative AI boom spend total of $3.1 trillion between 2023 and 2030, and $2.4 trillion in AI-focused R&D.

That's a total of $5.4 trillion in growth spending, primarily on AI, from five companies over a seven-year period.

Of course, these estimates could change in a hurry should demand for AI not keep growing, but for now, the best-run companies in history are all in on a $5.4 trillion spending plan that has analysts like Morgan Stanley and Grandview Research expecting AI spending by 2030 to reach 5.5% to 6% of GDP.

  • $1.4 to $1.8 trillion per year.

For context, the record infrastructure spending bill passed in 2021 was for $1.25 trillion over 10 years.

By 2030, economists and analysts expect that a handful of tech giants will be spending more than this every single year.

That's the kind of unprecedented investment boom we're seeing.

  • By 2031, potentially, a new record of over 6% of GDP could be spent on AI.
  • Surpassing the 6% record set by railroads in the 1880s.

Reason 3: Nvidia's Chip Utility Moat

Why is Nvidia the world leader in AI chips, even though everyone is racing to design competing chips?

The Three Pillars of Nvidia's Moat

1. CUDA (The Transmission Lines) – The Deepest Moat

The CUDA (Compute Unified Device Architecture) software architecture is like the iOS of AI. For almost two decades, the world's leading software programmers and research institutions have been building AI software on CUDA.

Nvidia is not just chips; it's a full-stack solution incorporating software.

This is a powerful network effect for Nvidia to maintain its industry lead because how many Facebooks are there?

The value of AI software is its universality. A company isn't going to create its own WhatsApp just for its employees and customers, and the same, so far, has been true for CUDA.

2. Hardware Dominance (The Generator)

Nvidia is sitting on over 90% market share in data center GPUs, which, with the B100/GB200 Blackwell architecture, is expected to maintain a lead ahead of its rivals.

And Blackwell Ultra is going to be available for order by the end of the year, the next generation that's even better, more efficient, faster, and lower long-term cost of operations per watt.

Nvidia is working to release a new architecture every year, and its rivals are not even able to match its current best-in-class offerings.

The Energy Imperative: Power Efficiency Rules The Future

Right now, the #1 constraint for hyperscalers is power. They need power, and that is Nvidia's ultimate value proposition.

When Jensen talks about 4X improvements each year, that can be split many ways.

4X faster for the same power (aka cost).

4X lower cost for the same performance.

2X better speed with 50% less power.

With power demand for AI expected to grow to levels that boggle the imagination.

According to the International Energy Agency (IEA), between 2026 and 2040, AI power demand is expected to grow from 37 GW to 11,000 GW.

How much power is that? In 2026 (37 GW), it's enough to power 26 million typical American homes.

It's almost half the peak summer demand of the state of Texas (85 GW).

It's the entire power demand of Sweden or Argentina.

And what about 2040? What is 11 TW of power?

It's enough to power 7.3 billion U.S. homes.

Over 33% of the 2040 global power is projected.

In other words, today, 9% (1/11th) of America's electricity is being used by AI datacenters.

By 2040, it might be 33%.

50% CAGR growth in AI power demand from 2026 to 2040.

5 GW = power used by New York City.

From 7 NYC's worth of power in 2026 to 2,200 NYC's worth of power within 14 years.

In 2023, the world had 3.4 TW of power-generating capacity.

By 2040, the IEA is estimating we're going to need that just for AI power demand (which includes robotics).

The next 14 years are a time period coinciding with Fundstrat's demographics-driven rise of automation.

Fundstrat thinks that the tech boom won't end for another 22 years, and even if they are wrong, the rise of AI and robotics is going to increase the demand for the kind of chips and systems NVDA is building exponentially.

What does all this mean? Simple. For a company like Microsoft, which hopes to be one of the world's leaders in cloud computing that powers all this, they need POWER EFFICIENCY ABOVE ALL!

For Microsoft, which signed a 20-year contract for all of the power from the 3 Mile Island Nuclear plant, efficiency is everything.

Because electricity is the limiting factor. They can buy anything else, GPUs, servers, build data centers to the moon, but without electricity, they can't make any money.

And that's why being the world leader in revenue per watt is going to be the #1 key to remaining a chip utility.

As long as Nvidia is the most efficient in terms of power, they are the lowest cost solution for the hyperscalers who are spending $5.5 trillion on AI between 2023 and 2030 (FactSet consensus).

Do you think Microsoft cares that Nvidia GPUs are $35,000 each? If they can save money long-term because of efficiency gains in one resource, they will NEVER HAVE ENOUGH OF?

  • 3X more power for AI by 2040 than the entire world uses today, per the IEA.

3. Networking (The Grid): Literally Putting It All Together

Nvidia has the most power-efficient AI GPUs in the world, BUT it's not just about GPUs. When Microsoft is building datacenters and spending tens of billions on them, they aren't just buying tens of thousands of Blackwell GPUs.

They are buying the full stack, including high-speed networking (Infiniband/NVLink).

Elon Musk has said he wants 1 million GPUs in the Collasus Mega cluster up and running by the end of the year to train Grok 6 (Grok 5 is already training).

And so do the hyperscalers.

A single "AI Factory", which includes several DGX Super Pods, can cost $1 billion.

THAT is the level of spending we're talking about.

The resources of entire nations are being marshaled by the world's largest and most profitable companies, who are in an all-out sprint to max out AI capacity.

And Nvidia is the one with full stop service, including the software subscriptions ($4,500 per GPU) to optimize all the hardware.

Do you think that a company like Meta, planning to spend $150 billion on the world's largest data center, which will consume power equal to NYC, is going to blink at $4,500 if it can save them money in the long term?

The Bullish Case For Nvidia

Tom Lee's 10X in 10 years bullish case for Nvidia (25% CAGR growth through 2035) is what I discussed in my last Nvidia report.

I do NOT think that this is actually possible (it would be $24 trillion per year of spending through 2040), BUT this is what the IEA is estimating is coming.

And now do you see why the hyperscalers (who likely estimate something smaller but still very large) are racing to build their own chips?

It's not because Microsoft, Alphabet, or Amazon think it will have more efficient chips; they need all the chips they can get!

Nvidia is supply-constrained for all of 2026, according to management.

And if these forecasts are even close to right (even within 10X of the truth), Nvidia could be supply constrained for the next decade (or 22 years per Fundstrat's 2047 scenario).

What does supply constraint mean? They sell every single bit of hardware Nvidia can make at full price.

There is no "air pocket" of demand. Its 50% FCF margins are not just spectacular but maintained.

  • That is the FactSet consensus through 2031.

Nvidia would become a chip utility.

50% FCF margins with 20% to 25% CAGR growth (management guidance) "for the foreseeable future".

And if the IEA's estimates are correct? Then Nvidia's growth could end up constrained only by what Taiwan Semi (TSM) can produce.

Imagine a WWII level of mobilization... that goes on for 22 more years (possibly longer).

That is what the AI boom is forecast to be, according to such reputable sources as Fundstrat and the IEA.

Nvidia Is Undervalued Right Now!

Nvidia is up 45% this year, an incredible run given the epic gains of 2023 and 2024.

Nvidia's estimates have been climbing all year and now point to a conservative 45% upside potential by the end of 2026.

That means after about 15X returns since 2022's lows, Nvidia has the fundamentals to potentially deliver another 45% one-year gain.

  • 21.75X return from 2022 lows if it happens.

That's 23% CAGR total return potential over the next three years, a 3X in 5 years, which is the Venture Capital rule of thumb.

But from Nvidia, the world's most valuable company.

That's how incredible this company's growth rates are.

Reason 5: The Medium-Term Growth Outlook

Nvidia's fundamentals are exceptional, including a 21% historical discount to fair value.

Risks To Consider: What Could Go Wrong

There are four risks to Nvidia's chip utility thesis.

  • In-house GPUs at hyperscalers.
  • Long-term sustainability of 75% gross margins.

Should China attack Taiwan or place a naval blockade, 90% of the world's advanced AI chip capacity would be taken offline.

That would be a significant hit to NVDA's results that year, and if a war were to occur in which China destroyed those TSMC factories, it would be a truly catastrophic event for NVDA investors.

A single fab, like the ones TSMC is building for 1nm chips, costs $20 billion to $25 billion.

In a full-scale Taiwan/China war, it would take 3 to 5 years to rebuild the capacity that TSMC currently has in Taiwan, should those fabs be destroyed.

And keep in mind that that's TODAY's demand. In 3 to 5 years, the natural demand for AI chips is expected to be significantly greater (about 100% more, according to Jensen Huang's guidance of 20% to 25% CAGR growth for the foreseeable future).

The 25% CAGR demand for AI chip growth for 5 years is 3X growth, so you can imagine the kind of significant fair value destruction that NVDA shareholders would undergo in such a worst-case scenario.

And what about the in-house silicon, such as Amazon's Trainium, Inferentia, and Graviton (server CPU) chips, which Anthropic is using to scale up to $10 billion to $100 billion in training runs?

Amazon's Trainium 2 GPUs are 30% to 40% lower cost than Hopper 100s.

In other words, most likely, Blackwell has a 1.4X to 2.1X revenue per watt advantage over Amazon's in-house chips at present.

And with Jensen guiding for a 1 million X improvement in 10 years (4X per year), that should allow Nvidia to remain ahead of Amazon, Alphabet, and, of course, rivals like Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and Intel (INTC).

However, if Jensen's law (4X improvement every year) doesn't hold, then Nvidia might end up losing enough market share that those 75% gross margins would decline.

  • It costs Nvidia $8,750 to build a $35,000 Blackwell GPU.

And if you believe the IEA and Fundstrat bullish cases for AI (the 11 TW of AI demand by 2040), then there is going to be a major backlash.

I put together a personal 45-page report for family and friends looking into the $6 to $7 trillion investment proposal that Sam Altman has proposed, and the results were not exactly optimistic about his ideas.

The proposal to invest trillions of dollars into Large Language model scaling turns out to be highly problematic,

A 2025 survey by the Association for the Advancement of AI (AAAI) found that 76% of AI researchers believe scaling current approaches is "unlikely" or "very unlikely" to succeed in creating AGI." Area.

LMMs are not likely to ever lead to AGI due to several issues, most notably a lack of grounding in the physical world and no ability to learn continuously.

That means that the projections and forecasts of companies like OpenAI and Anthropic are based on what 76% of AI experts consider to be hype (to put it kindly).

Do you think Sam Altman or Dario Amodai is going to tell us the truth about whether Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) or Artificial Super Intelligence (ASI) is right around the corner?

And that their $1 to $100 billion training runs on LMMs will get us there?

According to Dario Amodai of Anthropic, by 2033, AI will have possibly "solved" every branch of science.

That's the justification for spending up to $100 billion to train a single model.

The trouble is that the resources necessary to run such models, including power and water, are going to harm a lot of people.

And if the tech is fundamentally flawed? If a $100 billion LMM isn't AGI, a magic god machine that can solve all of humanity's problems?

Then we could see a HUGE pushback from voters.

If the AI boom turns into a source of social instability, then the U.S. government might change, with an anti-AI government swept into office that completely upends the entire AI boom.

That kind of "growth killed by its own success" is a risk that many investors don't think about but should.

Bottom Line: Nvidia Is Still A Strong Buy Before Earnings

I can't tell you how big the AI boom will get, or whether it proves to be a telecom-style bubble like 2000 (my guess is not).

The size of the boom? That's for the tech gods to decide;)

But what I can tell you is that right now, NVDA is absolutely not in a bubble (unless you think a 13X price increase isn't justified by a 16X increase in free cash flow ;)

As long as the AI boom continues, Nvidia is a supply constrained chip utility with very stable free cash flow growing at 20% to 25% (36% through 2028).

And given the 21% historical discount (even today!) and 66% upside potential through the end of 2026, Nvidia remains one of the lowest risk ways to profit from what may be the single greatest investment cycle in human history.

Will it be volatile? Yes, 60% more so than the S&P.

Will there be lots of scary headlines over the coming years?

You bet.

But as long as the hyperscalers are spending more on AI every year (FactSet consensus through 2030), all the scary headlines will be noise and buying opportunities.

The Most Important Table Nvidia Investors Will See All Year

As long as this table holds, Nvidia's thesis as a chip utility is intact.

As long as the AI boom continues and token counts are growing strongly, the hyperscalers will keep buying Nvidia's hardware.

"What about if the AGI hype is nonsense! Didn't you say LLMs can't become AGI!?"

Investing in NVDA is not a bet on the "magic god machines" that Amodei, Sam Altman, Zuckerberg, and Elon Musk are promising are coming soon.

It's a bet that the AI that exists now and the improved models of the next few years (through 2030) will be so useful that AI usage will grow, and the big AI spending will continue.

That means NVDA's value proposition isn't "The Godfather of AGI" but simply the chip utility that makes the hyperscalers more profitable every year.

That's the reason I like NVDA as a low-risk AI "play."

If OpenAI can't deliver AGI, its $500 billion market cap will collapse (or at least it will take years to grow into).

But hyperscalers are NOT betting on AGI. They are betting that today's AI systems will drive enough growth to justify their supply constrained datacenter buildouts through 2030.

Robotics alone will justify so much data center construction that NVDA is likely to remain supply constrained not just in 2026 but for the foreseeable future.

And that's why NVDA is not just my biggest position today (15% of my portfolio), but even in 2026, when the explosive growth will slow to "just" 36%, I plan to maintain a 7% to 7.5% target core allocation.

With limit orders set to take advantage and overweight from there, WHEN (not if) NVDA has a correction (and likely bear market) in 2026.

Because as long as the hyperscaler spending continues, the NVDA chip utility thesis is true, and as long as it's true, those nasty bear markets (a 37% decline in April) are always a great buying opportunity.

And what happens if the story changes? If the AI boom ends? Then I'll let you know in my quarterly Nvidia pre-earnings "I'm Buying More Nvidia And So Should You" articles. ;)

When the facts change, I change my mind. What do you do, sir?" John Maynard Keynes.

But for now, the best facts we have are clear. Nvidia is a potentially strong buy ahead of earnings.

Options markets are pricing in 7% to 10% up or down after earnings. I can't tell you what direction it will be.

I can tell you that the fundamentals Nvidia will deliver will be objectively excellent.

All the hyperscalers have doubled down on growth spending, so the results will be great.

What happens on August 27th? Great results; no idea about price reaction.

But what about Nvidia in the next year? Two years? Five years?

The data is clear.

To paraphrase my table pounding after Liberation Day:

Shut up and buy Nvidia today, and you'll thank me in a year. ;)

data.title: Cenovus And MEG Energy: Great Deal
data.description:
Article Thesis

Canadian energy company Cenovus Energy Inc. (NYSE:CVE) and MEG Energy Corp. (OTCPK:MEGEF)(TSX:MEG:CA) agreed on a deal that will see the former buy the latter for around CAD$7.9 billion. This deal increases Cenovus' scale, will allow for substantial synergies, and will, importantly, be accretive to Cenovus' per-share metrics from the beginning. All in all, I like this deal, and it looks like the market does as well -- Cenovus' shares rose by 8% following the announcement of the takeover.

Past Coverage

I have never written about MEG Energy before, but covered Cenovus Energy from time to time on Seeking Alpha. My most recent article on Cenovus Energy is from the beginning of this year, when I wrote this bullish article on CVE. Since then, shares have returned 14%, easily beating the broad market's 7% return over the same time frame. With the deal with MEG Energy being announced on Friday, it is time to update my views on the company.

What Happened?

While there had been rumors about a potential deal between Cenovus Energy and MEG Energy in recent weeks, we now have an official announcement: Cenovus Energy will buy MEG Energy for CAD$7.9 billion.

The deal will be paid via a mix of cash and new shares of Cenovus Energy, with MEG's shareholders receiving CAD$27.25 per share in total. MEG's shareholders can choose whether they want a cash payment or whether they want to be paid in shares of Cenovus Energy. Depending on how many shareholders choose the first or the second option, there will be a pro-rata payment, as the deal agreement sees a maximum total cash payment of CAD$5.2 billion and a maximum share issuance of 84.3 million shares. Depending on what the majority of shareholders decide and what every individual shareholder decides they want as payment, it is thus possible that individual shareholders get pure cash, only shares, or a mix between the two.

The deal was well-received by the market, with CVE rallying by around 8% on the day of the announcement, while MEG's shares were up slightly as well.

The two companies believe that the transaction will close during the fourth quarter of the current year, although there is, of course, no guarantee for that. While I do not think that it is very likely, it is possible that regulatory problems push the closing date into 2026.

CVE Buys MEG: Strategic Rationale

Cenovus is a much larger company compared to MEG Energy, as the following graph illustrates:

CVE's enterprise value of a little more than US$33 billion is around 6x as large as MEG Energy's enterprise value of US$5.7 billion, so this is a case of a much larger company acquiring a much smaller company. This has advantages, as it means that integrating the smaller company will not be an overly complicated task -- when the target is large, relative to the size of the acquirer, the integration process can become too burdensome and complicated. I believe that this won't be a problem here -- MEG's smaller size and more narrow operational focus mean that integrating it shouldn't be too complicated. At the same time, MEG isn't so small that acquiring it won't make a difference for Cenovus -- at around 17% of CVE's size (in terms of enterprise value), adding MEG will give a sizeable boost to CVE in terms of reserves, production, asset footprint, revenue generation, earnings, and cash flow.

Cenovus Energy is active across different segments of the energy industry, owning oil sands assets, offshore oil assets, refining assets, and so on. MEG Energy, due to its smaller size, is more focused -- MEG Energy is an oil sands pureplay. The following slide from the acquisition presentation shows that Cenovus Energy's oil sands assets and MEG Energy's oil sands assets are a great fit:

CVE's footprint and MEG's footprint in the Christina Lake region in the Alberta oil sands are directly neighbored, with MEG's footprint even being nested between the main portion of CVE's assets and some smaller areas that were previously "stranded".

Cenovus knows these assets very well, so there is, I believe, very little execution risk and very little risk of CVE overpaying due to them not accurately valuing MEG's assets.

Acquiring MEG Energy will add around 110,000 barrels of oil to Cenovus' daily production, which is around 14% of Cenovus' planned production for the current year; thus, this deal is meaningful enough to move the needle for Cenovus. The company explains that owning the land it currently owns and adding MEG's lands in the Christina Lake region will allow for production growth potential, as wells can be drilled more efficiently due to a smaller regulatory impact, due to CVE's improved technology being used on former MEG assets in the future, and so on. Between these tailwinds, the current MEG assets should see production growth of around 30,000 barrels of oil per day, or close to 30%, in the near term, with capital expenditures required for this additional production being low, according to management.

When two companies with a well-fitting asset footprint in a capital-intensive industry combine, it can be expected that there will be significant synergies. Cenovus' management believes that around CAD$150 million in synergies will be captured in 2026, between corporate cost savings and operating and development cost savings, but management guides for a much bigger CAD$250+ million in additional cost savings in the following years, primarily via operations and development becoming more efficient. In total, this means CAD$400+ million in synergies over a multi-year time frame, which seems very appealing. While CVE has not been as successful as it wished with past refinery takeovers, I think that execution risk here is small -- as noted above, CVE knows these oil sands assets extremely well.

Cost savings of CAD$400 million per year naturally mean a (pre-tax) earnings and cash flow boost of a similar magnitude, which will boost CVE's future profitability and cash generation potential nicely.

But not all of these synergies will be needed, per management, as Cenovus should see its adjusted funds flow and adjusted free funds flow grow in the first year, thanks to the acquisition being immediately accretive.

Debt And Shareholder Returns

So far, there are many things to like here -- Cenovus boosts its production and reserves nicely, via high-quality assets it knows well, and there is potential for massive synergies.

Unfortunately, the acquisition will mean lower shareholder returns in the future, though. Due to around 75% of the acquisition price being paid in cash, Cenovus has to take on around CAD$5 billion of debt, which will make its net debt grow substantially, to more than CAD$10 billion.

Cenovus' shareholder return framework sees the company put 50% of its free funds flow towards debt reduction and 50% of its free funds flow to shareholder returns as long as net debt is above CAD$6 billion, which will be the case following the closing of this deal. Shareholders thus will have to live with somewhat lower shareholder returns in the near term.

Is Cenovus A Good Investment?

While lower shareholder returns are a bit of a bummer, I like this deal nevertheless. In the long run, this acquisition makes Cenovus larger, stronger, and more efficient (synergies, better asset footprint), and boosts reserves and production nicely. Combined with the planned production growth from existing growth projects, Cenovus should be a much larger and much more profitable company a couple of years from now -- which will ultimately allow for very nice shareholder returns, even though those will not be strong in the very near term.

On a standalone basis, Cenovus Energy trades at just around 5x EBITDA, making it a pretty inexpensive stock despite its low break-even costs and nice growth outlook, which is why I was bullish on CVE even before the MEG deal. With that deal strengthening the company and being accretive in the first year, I remain bullish on CVE and the future CVE/MEG company.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

Returns for periods shorter than one year are cumulative, and results for other share classes will vary. Shares will fluctuate in value and, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance cited. Performance figures assume that all distributions are reinvested. For the most recent month-end performance, visit jhinvestments.com.

Market review and outlook

The world equity markets rebounded from initial weakness to register strong gains in the second quarter. Stocks fell sharply in early April in response to U.S. tariff announcements on concerns that Washington’s new approach to trade would lead to slower economic growth. Within a week, however, the U.S. administration reversed course and declared a 90-day pause on tariffs. The move fueled hopes that the initial plan was largely a negotiating tactic, providing investors with confidence that trade policy would ultimately be less restrictive than first thought. In addition, data related to global growth, inflation, and corporate earnings exhibited stability despite the shifting policy backdrop.

Equities recovered all of their earlier losses in the ensuing rally, propelling the major world indexes to all-time highs by the end of June. Growth stocks, lower- quality companies, and the mega-cap U.S. technology sector led the way higher, while the value style and defensive investments generally underperformed. This marked a pronounced reversal from the trends in place during the first quarter.

Bonds also delivered positive total returns. After experiencing volatility early in the quarter, the fixed-income markets recovered on the strength of low inflation, moderate growth, and expectations that central banks would continue to ease monetary policy. Credit-oriented market segments performed well in this environment and generally outpaced government debt.

Contributors and detractors

Asset allocation contributed to results, primarily as a result of our decision to maintain an overweight in stocks relative to bonds. An underweight in emerging market stocks and an overweight in international large-cap developed stocks also made a small contribution. On the other hand, an overweight in U.S. mid caps, which did not keep pace with large caps, had a modest, negative effect on results. Underlying manager performance detracted somewhat, with nearly all of the shortfall occurring in the equity portfolio.

Portfolio changes

The first half of the year was characterized by dramatic swings in the markets, both in terms of price movements and investors’ rapid shifts among specific asset classes. For our part, we don’t attempt to time the market or chase momentum in certain areas that happen to be outperforming in a given month. Instead, we remain focused on the core tenets of our investment process: fundamentals, valuations, and diversification. While we frequently make small changes to the portfolio, the moves are based on rigorous analysis and a three- to five-year outlook. In addition, we strive to keep the portfolio positioned for a broad range of outcomes instead of trying to predict the path of the economy or the direction of government policy. We believe this steady approach, rather than one that attempts to react to the ever-changing daily headlines, is the optimal way to help the fund achieve its longer-term investment objectives.
